1: יִשְׂמַח-מֶלֶךְ בְּעֻזְּךָ יְהוָה (yismah-melekh bĕʿuzzĕkā YHWH, "The king rejoices in your strength, LORD") — the royal psalm of thanksgiving following the victory anticipated in Ps 20. The king's rejoicing is grounded in ʿōz (strength): the same word as Psalms 18:1 (ḥizqî, "my strength") but now the divine strength specifically. וּבִישׁוּעָתְךָ מַה-יָּגִיל מְאֹד (ûbîshûʿātĕkā māh-yāgîl mĕʾōd, "in your salvation how greatly he exults!") — gîl: intense, even physical rejoicing (Psalms 19:5 used it of the sun's running); mĕʾōd: greatly, exceedingly.
2: תַּאֲוַת לִבּוֹ נָתַתָּה לּוֹ (taʾăwat libbô nātattāh llô, "You have given him his heart's desire") — the same concept prayed for in Psalms 20:4 is now reported as granted. Taʾăwāh: desire, longing — the deep wanting of the heart. Nātan (perfect: given, completed act). וַאֲרֶשֶׁת שְׂפָתָיו בַּל-מָנַעְתָּ (waʾăreshet śĕpātāyw bal-mānaʿtā, "have not withheld what his lips requested") — ʾăreshet: request, what the lips petition. Mānaʿ (to withhold, deny): not withheld. The paired statement: given what he desired, not withheld what he requested. Selāh.
3: כִּי-תְקַדְּמֶנּוּ בִּרְכוֹת טוֹב (kî-tĕqaddĕmennû birĕkôt ṭôb, "For you meet him with blessings of goodness") — qādam (piel: to come before, to meet in advance): God goes ahead of the king and meets him with blessings. Birĕkôt ṭôb: blessings of goodness/prosperity — not a single blessing but multiple. תָּשִׁית לְרֹאשׁוֹ עֲטֶרֶת פָּז (tāshît lĕrōʾshô ʿăṭeret paz, "you set a crown of finest gold upon his head") — ʿăṭāret: a crown, a wreath of honor; paz: refined gold, the purest gold. The divine coronation: God himself places the finest crown on the king's head, not the human coronation ceremony alone.
4: חַיִּים שָׁאַל מִמְּךָ נָתַתָּה לּוֹ (ḥayyîm shāʾal mimmĕkā nātattāh llô, "He asked you for life, and you gave it to him") — the king's petition for life (ḥayyîm, plural of fullness) was answered. אֹרֶךְ יָמִים עוֹלָם וָעֶד (ʾōrek yāmîm ʿôlām wāʿed, "length of days forever and ever") — the phrase ʿôlām wāʿed: forever and continuing — the most emphatic temporal expression. For a historical king, this is the prayer for dynasty; for the Messianic king, it is literal eternal life. Hebrews 1:8 cites the parallel Psalms 45:6 ("your throne, O God, will last forever and ever") for Christ; Psalms 21:4's language functions in the same register for NT readers.
5: גָּדוֹל כְּבוֹדוֹ בִּישׁוּעָתֶךָ (gādôl kĕbôdô bîshûʿātekā, "Through your salvation his glory is great") — the king's glory (kābôd: weight, honor, splendor) is great specifically "in your salvation" — the salvation is the source of the glory, not the king's own achievement. הוֹד-וְהָדָר תְּשַׁוֶּה עָלָיו (hôd-wĕhādār tĕshawweh ʿālāyw, "you have bestowed splendor and majesty upon him") — hôd (splendor, the divine majesty vocabulary of Psalms 8:1) and hādār (majesty, beauty) — the two terms of divine glory now conferred on the human king. The transfer of divine glory-vocabulary to the royal figure is one of the foundations of Messianic expectation.
6: כִּי-תְשִׁיתֵהוּ בְרָכוֹת לָעַד (kî-tĕshîtêhû bĕrākôt lāʿad, "For you make him most blessed forever") — shît (to place, set, make): God makes the king into a source of blessing (bĕrākôt: blessings, the formal benedictions). Lāʿad: for eternity, perpetually. תְּחַדֵּהוּ בְשִׂמְחָה אֶת-פָּנֶיךָ (tĕḥaddêhû bĕśimḥāh ʾet-pānêkā, "you fill him with gladness in your presence") — ḥādāh (piel: to make glad, to fill with joy); pānêkā: your face/presence. Joy in the divine presence as the supreme royal blessing.
7: כִּי-הַמֶּלֶךְ בֹּטֵחַ בַּיהוָה (kî-hammelekh bōṭêaḥ baYHWH, "For the king trusts in the LORD") — bāṭaḥ: to trust, to feel secure (the physical sense of leaning against something solid). The participle: ongoing, habitual trust. וּבְחֶסֶד עֶלְיוֹן בַּל-יִמּוֹט (ûbĕḥesed ʿelyôn bal-yimmôṭ, "through the steadfast love of the Most High he will not be shaken") — ḥesed ʿElyôn: the covenant loyalty of the Most High (the highest divine title). Māṭ (niphal: to totter, to be shaken): the king who rests in divine ḥesed cannot be toppled. Psalms 125:1 used the same image for the immovable mountain of Zion.
8: תִּמְצָא יָדְךָ לְכָל-אֹיְבֶיךָ (timṣāʾ yādĕkā lĕkol-ʾōyĕbêkā, "Your hand will reach all your enemies") — the address shifts to the LORD (second person): "your hand." The hand as the instrument of divine power that extends to find and reach every enemy. יְמִינְךָ תִּמְצָא שֹׂנְאֶיךָ (yĕmînĕkā timṣāʾ śōnĕʾêkā, "your right hand will seize those who hate you") — the right hand (yāmîn): the hand of power and authority (Psalms 18:35: the right hand held the Psalmist up). Here it is the right hand that finds the enemy — the divine arm extended in judgment.
9: תְּשִׁיתֵמוֹ כְּתַנּוּר אֵשׁ (tĕshîtêmô kĕtannûr ʾêsh, "You will make them like a burning oven") — tannûr: an oven/furnace (used for baking bread; also for smelting). The enemies as fuel for an oven at the time of the LORD's appearance (lĕʿêt pānêkā: "in the time of your face/presence"). יְהוָה בְּאַפּוֹ יְבַלְּעֵם (YHWH bĕʾappô yĕballĕʿêm, "the LORD in his wrath will swallow them up") — bālaʿ (piel: to swallow, to engulf): total destruction by being consumed. ʾAp (nostril/anger): the burning anger of Psalms 18:8.
10: פִּרְיָמוֹ מֵאֶרֶץ תְּאַבֵּד (piryāmô mêʾereṣ tĕʾabbêd, "You will destroy their offspring from the earth") — pĕrî: fruit, offspring; ʾābad (piel: to destroy, to cause to perish). The destruction is total and extends to the next generation. וְזַרְעָם מִבְּנֵי אָדָם (wĕzarʿām mibbĕnê ʾādām, "their descendants from among mankind") — zeraʿ: seed, offspring, descendants. Both terms (offspring and seed) cover the complete lineage.
11: כִּי-נָטוּ עָלֶיךָ רָעָה (kî-nāṭû ʿālêkā rāʿāh, "For they intended evil against you") — the reason for judgment: their intent (nāṭāh: to incline, to aim) was evil directed at the LORD himself, not merely at the king. The king's enemies are by that fact enemies of the LORD — the Davidic covenant means that attacking the king is attacking God's anointed. חָשְׁבוּ מְזִמָּה בַּל-יוּכָלוּ (ḥāshĕbû mĕzimmāh bal-yûkālû, "devised a plot they cannot carry out") — zāmam: to devise, plan (often with evil intent); mĕzimmāh: scheme/device. Their scheming cannot succeed.
12: כִּי-תְשִׁיתֵמוֹ שֶׁכֶם (kî-tĕshîtêmô shekem, "You will put them to flight") — shekem: shoulder/back (here used idiomatically for putting someone to retreat — "turning the shoulder/back"). בְּמֵיתָרֶיךָ תְּכוֹנֵן עַל-פְּנֵיהֶם (bĕmêtārêkā tĕkônên ʿal-pĕnêhem, "as you aim your bow at their faces") — mêtār: a bow-string; the image of aiming the bow directly at their faces.
13: רוּמָה יְהוָה בְּעֻזֶּךָ (rûmāh YHWH bĕʿuzzekā, "Rise in your strength, O LORD!") — the psalm ends with a call and a response. Rûm (hiphil imperative: to lift yourself up, to be exalted) echoing Psalms 3:7 (qûmāh YHWH) and Psalms 7:6 (qûmāh YHWH). נָשִׁירָה וּנְזַמְּרָה גְּבוּרָתֶךָ (nāshîrāh ûnĕzammĕrāh gĕbûrātekā, "We will sing and praise your mighty deeds") — shîr (to sing) and zāmar (to sing with instrumental accompaniment — the word from which mizmôr comes): the full liturgical response of the congregation to the divine strength. The psalm closes the pre-battle liturgy cycle (Ps 20-21) with a vow to sing after the victory God provides.
